narrative: Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated a 43% chance that you would not pass—above the threshold we use for admissions decisions. This prediction draws from your academic record, test scores, family background, and planned study schedule, comparing them to patterns seen in thousands of past students. The most influential factor pushing toward this higher failure risk was your choice to attend part-time, as the vast majority of students who passed—over 93%—went full-time, allowing more focus on studies without work distractions. In contrast, part-time paths have historically correlated with tougher odds of bar success in our data. On a positive note, your third-year decile ranking of 7 stood out strongly in your favor, better than the average of about 5.9 for those who passed, showing solid late-stage performance that pulled the prediction toward success. Similarly, your undergraduate GPA of 3.30 exceeded the 3.24 average of bar passers, giving another boost by signaling strong foundational skills. Your LSAT score of 40 and upper-middle family income also aligned well with successful profiles, though they played smaller roles here. Overall, while your grades and scores compare favorably to those who passed, the part-time commitment tipped the scales enough to raise concerns about bar preparation. This isn't a judgment on your potential—many factors like dedication and study habits can shift outcomes—but our model prioritizes these historical patterns to guide admissions. To strengthen a future application, consider switching to full-time if possible, or build evidence of bar readiness through targeted prep courses or higher recent grades. You might also retake the LSAT to push even further above averages, or seek letters highlighting your resilience as a part-time worker. Reapplying after addressing this key hurdle could make a real difference, as your other strengths position you well among admitted students. Keep pushing forward; your profile shows real promise with the right adjustments.
